claims.claim_text: Amazon's recruiting model effort started in 2014, scored candidates one to five stars, was trained on a decade of resumes, was found biased by 2015, penalized resumes containing "women's," and downgraded graduates of two women's colleges before being scrapped in 2018.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters (opening paragraph)
claims.rationale: Reuters reports these incident details, but Reuters is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The Amazon recruiting project caused an estimated write-off of over ten million dollars plus severe PR damage.
claims.anchor_text: It resulted in an estimated write-off of over ten million dollars in specialized compute time, countless engineering hours, and severe public relations damage.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The project was eventually abandoned after significant engineering effort and public scrutiny.
claims.rationale: The cited reporting supports the failure of the project, but not this precise cost estimate.

claims.claim_text: Stanford released Alpaca on March 14, 2023, using 52,000 text-davinci-003-generated instruction examples, for under $600, and it showed small open models could match proprietary ones.
claims.span_hint: section: The Big Picture: Why Fine-tune? (Did You Know box)
claims.anchor_text: On March 14, 2023, Stanford released the Alpaca model, which was fine-tuned on 52,000 instruction-following demonstrations generated by OpenAI's text-davinci-003. The entire fine-tuning process cost under $600 and proved that small open-source models could match proprietary models when given high-quality instruction data.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In March 2023, Stanford's Alpaca project helped popularize the idea that instruction tuning a small open model could be reproduced cheaply with synthetic instruction data.
claims.rationale: The sentence mixes off-allowlist sourcing, an incorrect date framing, and an overstated performance conclusion.

claims.claim_text: QLoRA combines LoRA with 4-bit quantization, including NF4, double quantization, and paged optimizers, to enable fine-tuning under tighter VRAM constraints.
claims.span_hint: section: QLoRA: Fine-tuning for Everyone (opening paragraphs and config block)
claims.rationale: S002 and S006 directly cover QLoRA's quantization method and implementation details.

claims.claim_text: Transformers PEFT integration supports non-prompt-learning adapters such as LoRA, IA3, and AdaLoRA, and requires `peft >= 0.18.0`.
claims.span_hint: section: QLoRA: Fine-tuning for Everyone > Hugging Face Integration
claims.rationale: Current Transformers PEFT docs explicitly list the supported methods and the minimum PEFT version.

claims.claim_text: When training PEFT adapters, Trainer checkpoints contain adapter-only artifacts such as `adapter_model.safetensors` and `adapter_config.json`.
claims.span_hint: section: QLoRA: Fine-tuning for Everyone > Hugging Face Integration
claims.rationale: The Transformers PEFT docs explicitly describe adapter-only checkpoints and name these files.

claims.claim_text: Adapter-only artifacts are always only tens of megabytes instead of gigabytes.
claims.span_hint: section: QLoRA: Fine-tuning for Everyone > Hugging Face Integration
claims.anchor_text: This precise isolation guarantees that your CI/CD pipeline outputs are just tens of megabytes in size rather than unwieldy gigabytes, heavily optimizing storage transmission overhead.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This precise isolation usually keeps artifacts far smaller than full-model checkpoints, reducing storage and transfer overhead.
claims.rationale: Adapter size varies with rank, target modules, model family, and save settings, so the exact size claim is illustrative rather than universal.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI currently documents four fine-tuning methods: supervised fine-tuning, vision fine-tuning, direct preference optimization, and reinforcement fine-tuning.
claims.span_hint: section: Proprietary Fine-Tuning: The OpenAI Ecosystem (opening paragraph)
claims.rationale: OpenAI's current fine-tuning guide enumerates these four methods.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI supervised fine-tuning data must be JSONL in chat-completions format, include at least 10 lines, and OpenAI recommends starting with about 50 well-crafted examples.
claims.span_hint: section: API Constraints and Data Formatting (paragraph 2)
claims.rationale: The supervised fine-tuning guide gives these exact formatting and minimum-example requirements.

claims.claim_text: Supervised fine-tuning checkpoints are generally only temporarily available and must be downloaded quickly.
claims.span_hint: section: API Constraints and Data Formatting (paragraph 2)
claims.anchor_text: Furthermore, checkpoints from supervised fine-tuning are often available temporarily, meaning you must rapidly download and rigorously validate your evaluation artifacts.
claims.suggested_rewrite: OpenAI saves only a limited set of recent supervised fine-tuning checkpoints, so inspect and evaluate them while the job context is still fresh.
claims.rationale: Current docs say only the last three epoch checkpoints are retained, not that checkpoints are broadly "temporary" in the way stated here.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI vision fine-tuning allows up to 50,000 image examples, up to 10 images per example, a maximum of 10 MB per image, JPEG/PNG/WEBP in RGB or RGBA, no assistant-role image outputs, and may exclude people, faces, children, or CAPTCHAs.
claims.span_hint: section: API Constraints and Data Formatting (vision constraints paragraph)
claims.rationale: The vision fine-tuning guide enumerates these exact limits, formats, and moderation exclusions.

claims.claim_text: GPT-4o-2024-08-06 introduced vision fine-tuning in August 2024.
claims.span_hint: section: API Constraints and Data Formatting (Did You Know box)
claims.anchor_text: In August 2024, the release of GPT-4o-2024-08-06 introduced Vision fine-tuning, allowing developers to upload up to 50,000 image examples (max 10MB per image) to natively train models on domain-specific visual tasks like medical imaging anomalies or autonomous driving analysis.
claims.suggested_rewrite: OpenAI later added vision fine-tuning for `gpt-4o-2024-08-06`, allowing developers to train on image-and-text examples for domain-specific visual tasks.
claims.rationale: OpenAI announced vision fine-tuning on October 1, 2024, not August 2024, and the numeric limits are already separately citable from the guide.

claims.claim_text: `TrainingArguments.evaluation_strategy` is the canonical field name across current Hugging Face stacks.
claims.anchor_text: The examples in this module use the canonical `TrainingArguments.evaluation_strategy` field name.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Training argument names and aliases can differ across library versions, so verify the exact field names in the versions of `transformers`, `trl`, and `peft` you actually run.
claims.rationale: Current Transformers docs prominently use `eval_strategy`, so calling `evaluation_strategy` the canonical name is not safe as a version-agnostic claim.

claims.claim_text: A healthcare startup mixed peer-reviewed dosage data with forum scrapes, caused a dangerous pediatric hallucination in clinical trials, and had to roll back the whole initiative.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories > Medical Hallucination at Scale
claims.anchor_text: A highly funded healthcare artificial intelligence startup attempted to heavily fine-tune a massive clinical classification model. To quickly expand their dataset volume, the engineers carelessly combined rigorously peer-reviewed pharmaceutical dosage papers with entirely unregulated, anecdotal health forum web scrapes. Because the core neural network inherently lacked the ability to differentiate the structural authority and scientific provenance of its diverse sources, the resulting fine-tuned system adopted a horrific, confident hybrid tone. During isolated clinical trials, the model confidently hallucinated a highly dangerous, unsubstantiated pediatric dosage recommendation derived entirely from a random internet post. The engineering team was forced to immediately execute a total production rollback, terminating the initiative and rewriting their entire ingestion pipeline.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Mixing authoritative medical sources with unvetted internet content can cause a fine-tuned model to produce dangerously overconfident outputs, so high-risk domains need strict dataset provenance and expert review.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed war story with concrete consequences and no citable source.

claims.claim_text: Bloomberg built a finance model by training from scratch and then aggressively fine-tuning it on decades of proprietary terminal data, proving a durable competitive moat.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories > Bloomberg's Custom GPT Architecture
claims.anchor_text: In contrast to catastrophic dataset failures, the engineering division at Bloomberg executed a masterful display of specialized domain adaptation. Recognizing that open-source models fundamentally struggled to parse the extremely dense, numerical syntax of professional financial tickers and proprietary market indicators, they committed to an immense undertaking. They initially trained a massive financial language model architecture practically from scratch, and then aggressively fine-tuned it explicitly on decades of their internal proprietary terminal data. This staggering operational achievement unequivocally proved that highly specialized domain fine-tuning serves as an impenetrable competitive moat, yielding a model capable of parsing complex financial sentiment with an accuracy that generic frontier models simply could not mathematically replicate.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Domain-specific language models can outperform general-purpose models on specialized financial tasks when they are trained on large, well-curated finance corpora, but the exact advantage depends on the data, model design, and evaluation setup.
claims.rationale: BloombergGPT was trained from scratch on mixed financial and public corpora; the paragraph's architecture and moat claims are not supported as written.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes uses device plugins for GPUs, exposes GPU resources such as `nvidia.com/gpu`, and requires GPUs to be specified in `limits` with matching requests behavior.
claims.span_hint: section: Deploying Training Jobs on Kubernetes (opening paragraph and manifest context)
claims.rationale: S009 directly documents device plugins, custom GPU resources, and the limits/requests rules.

claims.claim_text: For Kubernetes Jobs, `backoffLimit: 0` prevents automatic retries after failure, and `restartPolicy: Never` means the kubelet does not restart the failed container in-place.
claims.span_hint: section: Deploying Training Jobs on Kubernetes (backoffLimit explanation paragraph)
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes Job docs describe backoff behavior and note that `restartPolicy: Never` means the kubelet does not restart the failed container in that Pod.

claims.claim_text: Training GPT-3 required 3.14E23 FLOPS, cost over $4.6 million, and comparable LoRA adaptation now takes hours on a $5-per-hour cloud instance.
claims.span_hint: section: Cost Analysis and Kubernetes Orchestration (Did You Know box)
claims.anchor_text: Training the original GPT-3 model in 2020 required an estimated 3.14E23 FLOPS of compute, taking weeks on thousands of GPUs and costing over $4.6 million. Today, applying LoRA to a similarly sized open-source model requires less than 0.1% of the trainable parameters, taking just hours on a $5-per-hour cloud instance.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Pretraining frontier-scale foundation models historically required enormous compute budgets, while LoRA-style adaptation can cut trainable parameters dramatically and make much smaller adaptation runs far cheaper.
claims.rationale: The FLOPs figure is citable, but the mixed cost and modern price comparison are dated and not supportable as one precise claim.

further_reading.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary paper for frozen-base low-rank adapters and the trainable-parameter reduction claims used throughout the module.

further_reading.title: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for 4-bit fine-tuning, NF4, double quantization, and paged optimizers under constrained VRAM.

further_reading.title: TRL SFTTrainer
further_reading.why_relevant: Best practical reference for modern supervised fine-tuning workflows, PEFT integration, and trainer behavior in Hugging Face stacks.

further_reading.title: OpenAI Fine-Tuning Guide
further_reading.why_relevant: Current vendor reference for supported fine-tuning methods, workflow, and platform-specific constraints that changed after the original module was written.

notes: Excluded quiz questions, exercise steps, Mermaid/code blocks, and most purely pedagogical analogies per pipeline rules. Focused on prose/table claims that are dated, quantified, incident-based, version-sensitive, or vendor/platform specific.
